content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 13, 2010

Steve Vaus
12827 Corte Dorotea
Poway, CA 92064

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file no. A-10-084

Dear Mr. Vaus:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act.

QUESTION

Would your recording of a telephone message to voters by a "robocall" directed to a city council special ballot measure committee be considered a contribution to your city council election to the council position that is the subject of the recall?

CONCLUSION

No. Based on the facts presented, your appearance in the "robocall" for the recall committee is not a contribution to you.

FACTS

You are a candidate for the Poway City Council. You are running for a seat that might be vacated if the ballot measure to recall current city councilmember Betty Rexford succeeds. You founded the Committee to Recall Betty Rexford from her Poway City Council seat. When the recall qualified for the June 8, 2010 ballot, you resigned as chairman of that committee and announced your own candidacy.

The Committee to Recall Betty Rexford has asked you, as a prominent member of the community and as being associated with this committee, to record a message that will be

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-084
Page No. 2

broadcast in a "robocall." The message will say: "Hi, this is Steve Vaus urging you to vote YES on the recall. Remember to vote YES on the recall on June 8th to restore our trust in our local government." There is no mention of your candidacy, though you would, obviously, state your name. Additionally, per a local rule, candidates for Poway City Council can only receive contributions from individuals.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines a "contribution" as "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." (Section 82015.)

Regulation 18215 further defines the term "contribution" as follows:

"(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. A payment is made for political purposes if it is:

"(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure; or

"(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any agent thereof:

"(A) A candidate;

"(B) A controlled committee;

"(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state central committee, county central committee, assembly district committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or

"(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes, including, but not limited to, a political action committee established by any membership organization, labor union or corporation . . . ."

The definition for Contribution is restricted by several exceptions found in Regulation 18215(c). Among these exceptions to the definition is: "A payment made by a ballot measure committee for a communication in which the ballot measure supported or opposed by the committee is endorsed or opposed by a candidate, and the communication does not expressly advocate the nomination or election of the endorsing candidate or the defeat of an opponent of the endorsing candidate." (Regulation 18215(c)(7).)

File No. A-10-084
Page No. 3

The Commission has long included a recall within the definition of "measure" under
Section 82043. Therefore, a committee primarily formed to support or oppose the recall of an
elected officer is a ballot measure committee (see generally Regulation 18531.5); a
communication made by a ballot measure committee in coordination with a candidate is not a
contribution to that candidate if, as explained above, the communication does not expressly
advocate the nomination or election of the endorsing candidate or the defeat of that candidate's
opponent. Regulation 18225(b)(2) provides that a communication "expressly advocates" the
nomination, election or defeat of a candidate if it contains express words of advocacy (such as
"vote for," elect, "support," or "defeat") or, when taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election.

The message that you would record for the Committee to Recall Betty Rexford would
qualify as an expenditure as defined above. However, based on the facts you provided, even
though the committee's payment for the "robocalls" is in coordination with you and thus at your
"behest" (see Regulation 18225.7, under Regulation 18215(c)(7) it is not a contribution to you
because it does not expressly advocate your election. Furthermore, even though the
communication does expressly advocate the recall of the incumbent city councilmember who
you are running to replace, that councilmember is not your "opponent" in this election. Your
only candidate running against you to replace the incumbent in the event the incumbent is recalled. The vote on the recall itself is a separate election which, as
mentioned, is on a "measure" and not another candidate. On this basis, the committee's payment
for the recall is not a contribution to you, although the committee must appropriately report the
expenditure on its campaign forms.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5560.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl
